statement: The March 2026 Gallup/Vísir survey on the EU referendum showed a significant gender gap: 62% of women support continuing EU accession negotiations versus 22% opposed, while 52% of men support continuing versus 39% opposed. Women are thus considerably more pro-negotiation than men. This gender gap is consistent with historical patterns in Icelandic EU polling, where women have consistently been more favourable toward EU membership by 10–15 percentage points. The pattern may reflect different prioritisation of issues: men are more likely to cite fisheries sovereignty concerns, while women are more likely to prioritise economic stability and social policy alignment.
source_name: Gallup Ísland / Vísir — EU referendum survey, March 2026
caveats: These figures are for 'continuing negotiations' not 'supporting membership', so the gender gap on the ultimate membership question may differ. The survey had approximately 800 respondents, meaning gender subsamples are around 400 each with correspondingly wider margins of error.

related_entries.statement: The Gallup Þjóðarpúls survey conducted February–March 2026 (sample size approximately 9,958, participation rate 43.3%) showed 42% of Icelanders support EU membership and 42% oppose it, with approximately 16% undecided. This represents a statistical dead heat, consistent with the trend of gradually rising support from ~35% in 2022 to ~45% in late 2025, now stabilising. A separate question on the upcoming referendum found 52% favour continuing EU accession negotiations versus 48% opposed — a higher figure than membership support, reflecting voters who want negotiations to proceed without necessarily supporting the end result of membership.

The Gallup Þjóðarpúls survey on the EU referendum was conducted online from 19 February to 4 March 2026 with a total sample size of 1,898 respondents randomly selected from Gallup's opinion panel. The response rate was 42.7%. The survey asked two main questions: whether respondents support holding a referendum on continuing EU accession negotiations, and how they would likely vote. Key headline findings: 57% support holding the referendum, approximately 30% oppose, and roughly 12% are neutral or undecided. On voting intention if a referendum were held: support for resuming negotiations leads, but when asked about actual EU membership (as opposed to negotiations), the split is closer to 42%–42% with 16% undecided. The gap between support for negotiations and support for membership is a significant methodological distinction in interpreting the results.

citations.report_title: Landbúnaðarstefna ESB henti íslenskum landbúnaði illa

claims.claim_text: Í þjóðaratkvæðagreiðslunni verða tveir valkostir: já til áframhaldandi viðræðna eða nei.

citations.report_title: Þjóðin á báðum áttum um viðræður: 52% gegn 48%

claims.claim_text: Karlar eru andvígari þjóðaratkvæðagreiðslunni en konur.
Konur myndu fremur kjósa með framhaldi aðildarviðræðna við ESB en karlar.
Karlar eru fremur andvígir atkvæðagreiðslunni en konur.
